Review of GHO data fetching design in gho.R

Source: conversation-export.md

User

请帮我看看R文件夹下的gho.R，你认为这个fetch GHO data的流程，我设计的是否合适

Assistant

Let me read the gho.R file to understand the current implementation.

The user wants me to review the GHO data fetching flow in gho.R and give my assessment. Let me analyze the design carefully.

Key components to review: 1. .gho_get() - The core HTTP fetch with OData paging 2. gho_data() and .gho_build_url() - The public API and URL building 3. gho_indicators() and .gho_indicators_build_url() - The catalog endpoint 4. gho_dimensions(), gho_has_data(), gho_count(), gho_coverage() - Utility functions

  1. gho_clean() - The cleaning and tidying function

Looking at what works well here: the fail-soft pattern handles network issues gracefully by returning NULL with warnings rather than throwing errors, OData paging is correctly implemented through the @odata.nextLink mechanism, and the retry logic with timeout and backoff is consistently applied across .gho_get() and gho_count(). The fix for the cli::cli_warn() glue injection vulnerability using conditionMessage(e) is solid, and gho_count() appears to have proper timeout handling.

The indicator catalog is efficiently memoized at the session level, and gho_clean() uses helper functions to gracefully handle missing columns. The spatial_type inference from the area column is a nice usability feature with appropriate messaging.

A few things worth examining: gho_dimensions() fetches the entire dataset just to extract unique dimension values, which could be inefficient for large indicators—using $select to fetch only the relevant column would be much faster. Also, gho_count() appears to be printing the URL somewhere, which creates some redundancy since .gho_get() already does this, though this is intentional since gho_count() bypasses that function. There’s also a potential surprise where gho_clean() might trigger a network call through its indicator name resolution, even when called offline. Finally, when gho_data() encounters a NULL result, it returns an empty tibble without the expected GHO column structure, which could cause issues downstream.

Looking at other functions, gho_coverage() uses base R’s split() and vapply() for grouping operations rather than dplyr, which is a reasonable choice to avoid extra dependencies. The paging logic accumulates results by repeatedly combining lists and then binding them together, which could theoretically be memory-inefficient for very large datasets, though GHO responses are typically bounded so this isn’t a practical concern. I should verify that the OData syntax for filtering multiple areas using the in operator is actually valid.

OData v4 does support the in operator, so that’s correct. However, gho_dimensions() downloads the entire response when it only needs one column—it could be more efficient by using the select parameter to fetch just that specific dimension. The type check in gho_clean() using is.data.frame() is appropriate because simplifyVector = TRUE converts JSON object arrays to data frames, while empty arrays become empty lists. There’s also a potential inefficiency where gho_clean() calls .gho_resolve_indicator_name(), which in turn calls .gho_indicator_catalog() on every invocation—

though this is mitigated by caching after the first network request. The difference between .gho_get() returning an empty tibble with no columns versus .dsi_empty_clean() returning a typed 15-column empty tibble is intentional, since .gho_get() is a generic OData fetcher rather than something specific to the unified schema. The more significant issue is that gho_dimensions() fetches all data without using $select, essentially downloading every row of the indicator when it should be more selective.

There’s also a maintenance concern with gho_count() duplicating the httr2 request pattern from .gho_get() just to access the @odata.count field. This creates a burden if the request logic needs to change in one place but not the other. A better approach might be to have a lower-level function that returns the full response body, letting both functions use it without duplication.

The core design is solid for a lightweight OData client with good fail-soft behavior, retry logic, and paging support. The real issue is gho_dimensions() fetching all columns when it only needs one—using $select would be a meaningful performance win, especially with large datasets. I can confirm the URL builder properly handles multiple select columns by comma-joining them, which is valid OData syntax.

整体设计很扎实——fail-soft 模式、retry/timeout 配对、OData paging、session-level 缓存都做得干净。有几个值得讨论的点，一个算是真正的 bug，其余是设计权衡。

gho_dimensions() 没有用 $select（最值得改的地方）

# 现在：下载 indicator 的全部列
res <- .gho_get(paste0("https://ghoapi.azureedge.net/api/", indicator))

但 .gho_build_url() 已经支持 select 参数了。对于 NCDMORT3070 这类大 indicator，这会拉取数十万行的全部字段，只是为了取某一列的 distinct 值。应该改成：

url <- .gho_build_url(indicator, select = dimension)
res <- .gho_get(url)

这个改动是低风险的，但在实际使用时性能差异会很明显。

gho_count() 的 HTTP 调用是必要的重复

它不走 .gho_get() 是因为需要读 @odata.count 而不是 value，这在 AGENTS.md 里有记录。可以接受，但这意味着日后调整 timeout 或 retry 逻辑时需要同步两处。一个更干净的做法是抽出底层 .gho_perform(url) 返回 parsed body，让 .gho_get() 和 gho_count() 都调它——但对当前规模来说可能是 over-engineering。

gho_data() 失败时返回无列的空 tibble

if (is.null(res)) tibble::tibble() else res

gho_clean() 失败时走 .dsi_empty_clean() 返回有类型的 15 列空 tibble，而 gho_data() 失败时返回 0×0 的空 tibble。这是有意为之（gho_data() 的 schema 由 GHO 原始字段决定，不是统一 schema），但如果用户做 gho_data(...) |> gho_clean() 遇到网络故障，gho_clean() 能正确处理 0 行输入，所以实际上不会出问题。这点是设计上的不对称，算是可接受的 trade-off。

其他几个小设计确认是合理的：

  • area 给了但 spatial_type 没给时自动推断并发 cli_inform 提示 — 这个 UX 很好
  • val <- body$value 用 is.data.frame(val) 检查（而不是 length > 0）是正确的，因为 simplifyVector = TRUE 时空数组返回 list() 不是 data.frame
  • contains(tolower(...)) 的 OData 搜索用单引号转义处理是正确的

总结

最值得提交一个修复的是 gho_dimensions() 缺少 $select。其余的设计权衡都有合理依据，维持现状没问题。

修复 gho_dimensions() 加上 select = dimension 参数。

考虑把 HTTP 调用逻辑抽到低层 .gho_perform() 以消除 gho_count() 的重复。

On this page

Developed by Shanlong Ding.
